skippedSources.name: a16z '5 Essential Books for Founders' note

items.title: Hormuz cumulative supply loss exceeds 1 billion barrels; ~30 Chinese ships allowed transit
items.idea: The total supply removed from global markets since the Hormuz closure has crossed 1 billion barrels — a figure with no modern precedent. Iran is selectively allowing approximately 30 Chinese vessels through while blocking everyone else, effectively turning the strait into a geopolitical toll road. G7 Finance Ministers meet in Paris today with Hormuz as the top agenda item. Trump is threatening Iran publicly while Iran rejects the US 5-point proposal outright, demanding sovereignty over Hormuz plus reparations as preconditions.
items.angle: The 1B barrel figure is the tweet. The China carve-out is the second-order story nobody is pricing: Iran is deepening its dependency on Beijing while the rest of the world queues.
items.tweetA: 1 billion barrels. That's the cumulative supply loss from Hormuz. This isn't a disruption anymore — it's a structural hole in global supply. Meanwhile Iran is letting ~30 Chinese ships through while blocking everyone else. Not an oil story. A China leverage story.
items.tweetB: Iran rejected the US 5-point proposal. Sticking to Hormuz sovereignty + reparations as preconditions. G7 meets in Paris today with Hormuz #1 on the agenda. Trump threatening "nothing left of them." This is not resolving this week. Price oil accordingly.

items.title: WTI $107.26 Sunday night +1.8%; last week WTI +11%, Brent +8.1% — week 12 of Hormuz closure
items.idea: Oil is gapping higher into Monday open: WTI at $107.26 (+1.8%), Brent at $110.47 (+1.1%). This follows WTI's best week in months (+11%), marking week 12 of the Hormuz closure without resolution. Aramco's CEO Nasser said market normalization now slips into 2027 if the strait remains blocked past mid-June — a 30-day countdown. Historical context: '10 of the last 12 recessions were preceded by an oil spike' (Niles).
items.angle: $107 WTI is not yet at demand-destruction levels for the US consumer but it's close. The lag to pump prices is 6-8 weeks — meaning June and July gas prices are already baked in.
items.tweetA: WTI $107 Sunday night. +11% last week. Week 12 of Hormuz. Aramco says normalization slips to 2027 if not resolved by mid-June. We have 30 days. Niles: '10 of last 12 recessions preceded by oil spike.' The clock is running.
items.tweetB: Oil up 11% last week, gapping higher Monday. The lag between WTI at $107 and gas pump pain is 6-8 weeks. June/July consumer prices are already baked in. The political pressure on Warsh at the June 16-17 FOMC is going to be extraordinary. $USO $XLE

items.title: CME FedWatch HIKE odds hit 45%; 10Y at 1-year high 4.595%; 30Y at 5.121% Oct 2023 high
items.idea: Rate hike probability has crossed 45% on CME FedWatch, while Polymarket is pricing 71% odds of NO cuts in 2026 at all. 10Y closed at 4.595% (1-year high), 30Y at 5.121% (Oct 2023 highs). Ed Yardeni: 'macroeconomic backdrop no longer supports easing bias, let alone rate cut.' Kevin Warsh's first FOMC is June 16-17, with Trump publicly pressuring him for cuts.
items.angle: The gap between equities (still near record highs before Friday's reversal) and bonds (pricing hikes) is the core tension. That gap doesn't close gently.
items.tweetA: CME FedWatch: 45% HIKE odds. Polymarket: 71% NO cuts in 2026. 10Y at 1-year high 4.595%. 30Y at Oct 2023 highs. The bond market repriced in 3 weeks what equities are still processing. That gap closes badly. $TLT $SPY
items.tweetB: Three months ago the market was pricing 3 Fed cuts in 2026. Today it's pricing a hike. Yardeni: 'backdrop no longer supports easing bias.' Warsh inherits this Friday. His first test is June 16-17. He gets $107 oil, 6% PPI, 3.8% CPI, and Trump calling for cuts. Good luck.

items.title: Tanker traffic in Hormuz near-zero; SPR draining at fastest rate ever — July fuel crisis incoming
items.idea: MacroEdge's Don Johnson reports virtually no tanker traffic in Hormuz, with a tolling system churning boats through too slowly. SPR is being drawn down at the fastest rate on record, and at current drainage rates, the US faces massive issues with fuel and distillates by July. Motor oil suppliers are already warning of delivery difficulties. Johnson: Iran has no incentive to deal right now — Hormuz is their maximum leverage point.
items.angle: The SPR is a finite buffer being burned faster than ever. July is 6 weeks away. The administration is betting on a deal before then. Distillates are the transmission mechanism to agriculture and trucking costs.
items.tweetA: MacroEdge: tanker traffic in Hormuz is near-zero. SPR draining at the fastest rate ever. Motor oil suppliers warning of delivery difficulties NOW. At current pace, massive fuel and distillate issues arrive in July. The administration is playing chicken with a 6-week runway.
items.tweetB: Iran's goal: find the WTI price that causes max US consumer pain while still exporting product. MacroEdge says we're not at demand-destruction levels — meaning Iran's leverage is still growing. The SPR drawdown is the tell: the US is quietly terrified and running out of buffer. $CL_F

items.title: Nvidia Q1 earnings Wed May 20: $78.8B revenue expected, $86.6B Q2 guide needed; SOX 32% above 50DMA
items.idea: Nvidia reports Wednesday after close. Street consensus: $78.8B Q1 revenue, $86.6B Q2 guidance. Citi says Nvidia needs to match or beat the Q2 guide to avoid a stock derate. Broader risk context: SOX is 32% above its 50-day moving average — historically widest premium. Wolfe Research flags AI tech as 'feeling toppy.' Nvidia closed Friday at $227.27 (-4.4%).
items.angle: The number that matters is not Q1 — it's the Q2 guide. $86.6B+ keeps the bull case alive. At 32% above 50DMA, the index doesn't need a bad print to correct — it just needs the print to not be extraordinary.
items.tweetA: Nvidia Wednesday. The number isn't Q1 — it's the Q2 guide. $86.6B+ keeps the bull alive. Less than that and Citi says derate. Buried context: SOX is 32% above its 50DMA — the widest premium historically. A beat doesn't prevent reversion from there. It just delays it. $NVDA $SOXX
items.tweetB: NVDA closed -4.4% Friday heading into earnings. SOX 32% above 50DMA. Wolfe: 'toppy.' The positioning is the problem — not the business. Nvidia's revenue is almost certainly fine. The question is whether fine is enough when you're the second-largest asset class in the world.

items.title: SpaceX reportedly filing public IPO as soon as Wednesday — ahead of Elon's birthday
items.idea: SpaceX is reportedly preparing to file IPO paperwork as early as Wednesday this week. This would make it the second major AI-adjacent IPO alongside OpenAI's planned H2 IPO and xAI's potential listing. The IPO would land with WTI at $107, 10Y at a 1-year high, and equities having just put in a blow-off top reversal on Friday.
items.angle: Three of the most valuable private companies (SpaceX, OpenAI, xAI) could go public in the same 12-month window. The supply of new equity issuance into a bond-stress environment is secondary pressure on valuations.
items.tweetA: SpaceX reportedly files IPO as soon as Wednesday. OpenAI targeting H2. xAI in the queue. Three of the most valuable private companies in history going public in the same window, into $107 oil, 4.6% 10Y, and a Friday blow-off top. The 2026 IPO calendar is either the best trade or the worst timing.
items.tweetB: SpaceX IPO filing Wednesday means the market absorbs one of the most complex valuation events since Aramco — in a week when Nvidia reports and the 10Y is at a 1-year high. The price discovery for 'what is a rocket company worth in an AI world' is not going to be calm.

items.title: Gold -2.7% and silver -8% Friday alongside stocks — liquidity event, not thesis failure
items.idea: Gold closed ~$4,553 after -2.7% Friday; silver -8% on heavy liquidation. This happened simultaneously with equities falling — a classic liquidity event where margin calls force selling of gains. BTIG's Krinsky characterized it as a 'liquidity event signature.' Historical analog: March 2020 saw the same pattern before the flight-to-quality bid returned within 3 days.
items.angle: Gold sold off because people needed cash, not because the inflation/Hormuz thesis failed. Oil is up $2 Sunday night. The dip is the setup.
items.tweetA: Gold -2.7%, silver -8% Friday while stocks fell. That's not gold breaking — that's margin calls. Forced liquidation sells what has gains. The inflation thesis (oil $107, PPI 6%, Hormuz week 12) hasn't changed. The bid returns when the dust settles. $GLD $SLV
items.tweetB: Gold and silver dumped WITH equities Friday = liquidity event, not gold thesis failure. This exact pattern happened March 13, 2020. Gold was back at highs within 3 weeks. The asymmetry from $4,550: upside if inflation stays hot, sideways if it doesn't. Downside requires a resolved Hormuz. $GC_F

items.title: BTC breaks below $80K weekend support; Fear & Greed at 31; THORChain $10M exploit; DeFi hack tally $35M+ May
items.idea: Bitcoin broke below $80,000, currently ~$78,180 (-1.11%). Fear & Greed index hit 31 — first sustained Fear reading in 6 weeks. THORChain suffered a $10M cross-chain exploit, RUNE -15%, protocol halted. May 2026 DeFi hack tally: $35M+ across THORChain, KelpDAO, TrustedVolumes. ETH at $2,180, SOL at $86.
items.angle: The $76K level is the floor to watch — a break brings a $72K liquidation cascade. Macro risk-off (rates up, oil up) plus DeFi-specific hacks is a double headwind not yet fully reflected in positioning.
items.tweetA: BTC broke $80K support over the weekend. Fear & Greed at 31. THORChain $10M exploit. DeFi May hack tally: $35M+. The floor to watch is $76K — Princeton Chen's note: break there = $72K liquidation cascade. This is a risk-off crypto environment, not a crypto-specific story.
items.tweetB: THORChain halted after $10M exploit. RUNE -15%. DeFi has now lost $35M+ to hacks in May alone. The structural security problem in bridging protocols hasn't been solved — it's being re-discovered every quarter. Cross-chain remains the highest-risk layer of the stack. $RUNE

items.title: Kevin Warsh inherits the Fed at worst possible moment: 6% PPI + 3.8% CPI + Hormuz shut + Trump demanding cuts
items.idea: Kevin Warsh officially takes control of the Federal Reserve with his first FOMC on June 16-17. He inherits: PPI at 6%, CPI at 3.8%, 10Y at a 1-year high, WTI at $107, CME hike odds at 45%, and a president openly calling for rate cuts. The June FOMC is now the single most consequential policy meeting of the cycle.
items.angle: Warsh is a known hawk. Trump's public pressure campaign for cuts is designed to give Warsh political cover to do what the data already demands. The irony: Trump's own Hormuz escalation is driving the inflation that makes cuts impossible.
items.tweetA: Warsh steps in as Fed Chair with: 6% PPI, 3.8% CPI, $107 oil, 4.6% 10Y, 45% hike odds, and the president publicly demanding cuts. His first FOMC is June 16-17. The last time a Fed Chair caved to political pressure in an inflationary environment was Arthur Burns, 1972. That ended in 21% rates.
items.tweetB: The Trump-Warsh dynamic: Trump's own Hormuz escalation is causing the oil-driven inflation that makes cuts impossible. Then Trump blames the Fed for not cutting. Warsh has to untangle this in 4 weeks. $TNX $DXY

items.title: Rate hike odds meaningfully priced in; summer data will decide: 1973 analog or 2022 repeat
items.idea: MacroEdge's Don Johnson writes that hike odds are now 'meaningfully priced in' and summer will be the deciding period. April rents increased YoY for the first time since the 2022 inflation wave, removing the key deflationary cushion from CPI. Without demand destruction (not yet triggered at current price levels), inflation continues accelerating. Johnson draws an explicit 1973 parallel.
items.angle: The rent reversal is the new data point — shelter was the CPI moderator for 18 months and it just turned. The 1973 analog implies multi-year structural inflation with a Fed forced to hike into a slowing economy.
items.tweetA: April rents up YoY for the first time since the 2022 inflation wave. Shelter was the CPI moderator for 18 months — and it just turned. Add $107 oil and 6% PPI. MacroEdge is drawing the 1973 parallel explicitly. Either this summer is a 'fakeout' or it's the second wave. The next 90 days decide.
items.tweetB: MacroEdge: hike odds will keep rising WITHOUT demand destruction, and we're nowhere near those commodity price levels yet. The summer tells us if inflation is cyclical or structural. The bond market is already pricing the latter. Equities have not.

items.title: Berkshire Q1: tripled GOOGL, bought $2.6B Delta, exited Amazon, Visa, Mastercard, UNH, Domino's
items.idea: Berkshire's Q1 13F shows sharp repositioning: total portfolio trimmed $274B to $263B, but GOOGL tripled. New $2.6B position in Delta. Exits from Amazon, Visa, Mastercard, UNH, Domino's. Apple still 21.99%. Princeton Chen reads the barbell: 'AI quality (GOOGL) + Delta cyclical + cash.'
items.angle: The Visa/MC exit is the most interesting call: Buffett is selling payment networks while the market treats them as untouchable quality. The GOOGL triple is a statement that AI revenue monetization is real and coming.
items.tweetA: Berkshire tripled GOOGL. Bought $2.6B Delta. Sold Amazon, Visa, Mastercard, UNH, Domino's. The Buffett barbell: AI monetization (GOOGL) + travel recovery (DAL) + cash. He's not buying chips — he's buying the thing that monetizes AI at scale. That's the frame. $GOOGL $DAL
items.tweetB: Berkshire exiting Visa and Mastercard while adding GOOGL is the most contrarian signal in Q1. V/MA are payment rails. GOOGL is an AI tollroad. If Buffett thinks stablecoins + CLARITY Act disrupts card rails long-term, the exit is early but not wrong. $GOOGL $DAL $V $MA

items.title: BlackRock: bond market regime shift = permanent duration risk premium; 60/40 structurally broken
items.idea: BlackRock is calling the current bond market dynamics a structural regime shift. Duration risk premium is now permanent, driven by fiscal deficits, geopolitical supply disruptions, and deglobalization. $10T+ AUM means this signal drives pension fund behavior globally. If duration risk is permanent, the bond-equity correlation that made 60/40 work for 40 years has flipped.
items.angle: BlackRock's framing is the institutional permission slip for pension funds to underweight duration structurally. Even 5% of global AUM moving from long bonds to alternatives creates sustained Treasury selling pressure independent of Fed decisions.
items.tweetA: BlackRock: bond market regime shift = permanent duration risk premium. Not a call. A structural asset allocation statement from the $10T manager. If they're right, the 40-year bond bull ended in 2021 and we're 4 years into repricing. 60/40 stopped working permanently. $TLT $AGG
items.tweetB: If BlackRock calls duration risk premium 'permanent' — pension funds start structurally underweighting long bonds. $10T of AUM repricing duration exposure is not a Treasury market footnote. That's the new marginal seller. $TNX $TMF

items.title: Empire State Manufacturing surged to 19.6 vs 7.0 est — 4-year high; economy not rolling over
items.idea: Empire State Manufacturing Index printed 19.6 Friday vs 7.0 estimate — a 4-year high and massive beat. The economy is not rolling over despite $107 oil and 6% PPI. This removes the 'soft landing' narrative justification for cuts. No natural mechanism for inflation to self-correct without Fed action.
items.angle: A 19.6 Empire State print while hike odds are at 45% and oil is at $107 is a stagflation false-positive: this looks like acceleration, not stagnation. But the oil transmission takes 6-8 weeks — by July, this print looks very different.
items.tweetA: Empire State Manufacturing: 19.6 vs 7.0 est. 4-year high. The economy is NOT breaking down. That's why this inflation setup is more 1973 than 2022 — demand is sticky. The Fed has zero cover to cut, and the data is actively making the case for a hike. $SPY $DXY
items.tweetB: Strong manufacturing data + $107 oil + 45% hike odds + 4.6% 10Y = the macro is sending one clear signal that equities are still arguing with. Empire State at a 4-year high is not deflationary. The lag to consumer pain is 6-8 weeks. We'll see this print differently in July.

items.title: Big 4 hyperscaler 2026 capex: $700B+ combined; Microsoft $190B, Amazon $200B
items.idea: The four largest hyperscalers have committed $700B+ combined 2026 capex for AI infrastructure. Microsoft $190B, Amazon $200B. These numbers exceed the GDP of many mid-sized economies deployed in a single year. The scale of fixed-cost commitment means margin pressure if AI revenue disappoints would be historic.
items.angle: $700B capex creates multi-year demand certainty for power equipment, networking, and cooling. It also creates a cliff: if AI monetization disappoints, these are the largest fixed-cost bases in corporate history. Bull and bear case are the same number.
items.tweetA: Big 4 hyperscalers: $700B+ combined capex in 2026. Microsoft $190B. Amazon $200B. That's more than the GDP of Belgium, deployed into AI infrastructure in 12 months. The power grid, cooling, and networking buildout is the trade — not just the chips. $VRT $ETN $ANET $VST
items.tweetB: $700B in hyperscaler capex creates 2 things simultaneously: the most certain multi-year demand signal in industrial infrastructure history, AND the largest fixed-cost exposure in corporate history if AI monetization disappoints. The bull and bear case are the same number.

items.title: SandDisk Q2 guide $7.7-8.3B vs $5.9B Q1 — 30-40% sequential guide-up, largest in memory history
items.idea: SandDisk issued Q2 revenue guidance of $7.7-8.3B versus $5.9B reported in Q1 — a 30-40% sequential guide-up in a single quarter. One of the largest sequential revenue guide-ups in memory industry history. Signals AI training and inference demand for NAND and DRAM is accelerating into Q2.
items.angle: SandDisk guides +30-40% while everyone's eyes are on NVDA. Memory is the quiet winner of AI buildout. If NAND demand is up 30-40% sequentially, the entire data center storage trade reprices. $MU $WDC
items.tweetA: SandDisk guided $7.7-8.3B Q2 vs $5.9B Q1. That's a 30-40% sequential guide-up — one of the largest in memory history. Everyone's watching NVDA Wednesday. Nobody's watching the memory blowout that tells you how big the AI buildout actually is. $MU $WDC
items.tweetB: Memory is the tell for AI infrastructure demand. SandDisk Q2 guide +30-40% sequential. If NAND and DRAM are getting pulled this hard, the hyperscaler buildout is not slowing — it's accelerating through Q2. The AI capex cycle has at least 2 more years of legs. $MU $STXS

items.title: NVDA crossed silver as world's 2nd-largest asset class; larger than all private equity combined
items.idea: Nvidia crossed the silver market cap last week, becoming the world's second-largest asset class behind only gold. Larger than all private equity combined, larger than every sovereign bond market except US and EU. Either AI is the most transformative technology in history, or this is the biggest valuation bubble in a generation.
items.angle: When a single company becomes an asset class, its volatility becomes systemic risk. If NVDA corrects 20%, the second-largest asset class is in correction. Pension funds and sovereigns forced in by index weight now have mandatory exposure to a binary earnings event.
items.tweetA: Nvidia is now the world's second-largest asset class. Larger than silver. Larger than all private equity. Larger than most sovereign bond markets. Either AI is the greatest wealth creator in history, or index-weight forced buying just made every pension fund massively long a single binary event. Wednesday will be interesting.
items.tweetB: NVDA crossed silver last week. Silver has been money for 5,000 years. Nvidia has been public for 24. The valuation implies AI does to the global economy what electricity did — in the next 5-10 years. That might be right. But $227 into a SOX at +32% 50DMA premium is not the entry. $NVDA

items.title: OpenAI reorg: Greg Brockman takes product control; ChatGPT + Codex + Atlas browser merging into super app
items.idea: OpenAI is merging ChatGPT, Codex, and Atlas browser into a unified 'super app' desktop application. Brockman officially leads all product strategy. Thibault Sottiaux (built Codex into OpenAI's fastest-growing product) takes core product/platform lead. Nick Turley, who grew ChatGPT to 900M WAU, moved to enterprise. Ashley Alexander (Instagram VP) leads consumer. Reorg triggered in part by Anthropic's momentum with Claude Code and Claude Codex in enterprise/developer space.
items.angle: The Codex-ChatGPT merger is a direct response to Anthropic's developer traction. The super-app ambition (chat + code + browser) is the same bet Google and Microsoft made. The differentiator is whether OpenAI can maintain developer loyalty while pivoting.
items.tweetA: OpenAI is merging ChatGPT + Codex + Atlas browser into one super app. The trigger: Anthropic's Claude Code is winning enterprise developer share. Brockman officially takes product. The guy who grew ChatGPT to 900M WAU just got moved to enterprise. OpenAI is now officially a developer-first company.
items.tweetB: OpenAI reorganization count in 2026: this is the second major reorg. Each triggered by a competitor. Google triggered Code Red #1. Anthropic's Claude Code triggered this one. The pattern says OpenAI is reactive, not leading. That's a moat question going into the IPO.

items.title: ChatGPT at 900M weekly active users — approaching TikTok scale; OpenAI IPO coming H2 2026
items.idea: Nick Turley grew ChatGPT to 900 million weekly active users before being reassigned to enterprise. TikTok peaked around 1.7B monthly actives. ChatGPT at 900M WAU may be the fastest platform to reach near-TikTok scale in history. OpenAI is 'hoping to simplify product offerings ahead of its plan to file for an IPO, which could happen later this year.'
items.angle: 900M WAU is the number. At any reasonable monetization rate, the revenue path to $300B+ valuation is visible. Whether enterprise coding (Codex) or consumer chat (ChatGPT) is the primary multiple driver is the IPO narrative battle the reorg is trying to settle before roadshow.
items.tweetA: ChatGPT has 900 million weekly active users. TikTok's peak was 1.7B monthly. ChatGPT might be the fastest platform to reach near-TikTok scale ever. The IPO is coming H2. The valuation question isn't 'is it worth it' — it's 'at what price does the revenue math close.' At $150B? Yes. At $300B? Maybe.
items.tweetB: 900M weekly ChatGPT users and OpenAI is still in Code Red mode because Anthropic is winning enterprise developers. The fundamental IPO tension: consumer product is massive but revenue model is enterprise. The multiple you assign depends entirely on which story you believe.

items.title: CLARITY Act advances 15-9 through Senate Banking Committee; Citi $143K BTC target predicated on passage
items.idea: The CLARITY Act passed Senate Banking Committee 15-9 — a bipartisan vote moving to full Senate. Citi has a $143,000 Bitcoin price target predicated on its passage, which would establish the first comprehensive US regulatory framework for digital assets, clarifying SEC vs CFTC jurisdiction and unlocking institutional custody and lending products.
items.angle: A 15-9 committee vote with bipartisan support is materially higher than market expectations. If passage odds moved from 30% to 60%, the probability-weighted price impact alone is not priced into BTC at $78K.
items.tweetA: CLARITY Act: 15-9 Senate Banking Committee. Bipartisan. The first US comprehensive crypto framework just cleared its biggest obstacle. Citi's $143K BTC target is predicated on this passing. The probability-weighted price impact alone is not in BTC at $78K. $BTC
items.tweetB: CLARITY Act passes Banking Committee 15-9. This unlocks institutional custody products, lending, and derivatives frameworks. The $78K BTC price doesn't reflect this. Either the vote doesn't pass the full Senate, or this is the dip to buy before the regime shift. $BTC $ETH

items.title: Bitwise launches spot Hyperliquid (HYPE) ETF — first native on-chain perp DEX to get institutional wrapper; +23% week
items.idea: Bitwise launched a spot ETF on Hyperliquid's native token (HYPE) — the first time a decentralized perpetual exchange infrastructure token received institutional ETF treatment. HYPE was up 23% on the week. Hyperliquid is the leading on-chain perp DEX with $3B+ daily volume, running fully on-chain without an off-chain matching engine.
items.angle: The ETF wrapper normalizes DeFi infrastructure exposure for TradFi allocators who cannot custody crypto directly. This is the template for DeFi infrastructure ETFs — after BTC and ETH, the next layer is DeFi protocols.
items.tweetA: Bitwise just launched a spot ETF on Hyperliquid (HYPE). The first native on-chain perp DEX to get institutional wrapper treatment. HYPE +23% on the week. This is what DeFi infrastructure going mainstream actually looks like — not retail, institutional allocation with familiar packaging. $HYPE
items.tweetB: A spot ETF on a DeFi perp DEX token launched this week. Six months ago that sentence was science fiction. The institutionalization of on-chain infrastructure is ahead of schedule. After BTC and ETH ETFs, the next layer is DeFi protocols — and HYPE just became the template. $HYPE $BTC

items.title: MSTR $1.5B 2029 convertible buyback funded by cash or Bitcoin sales — de-leveraging signal
items.idea: MicroStrategy announced a $1.5B buyback of its 2029 convertible notes to be funded by cash or Bitcoin sales — a reversal of its core playbook (issue converts → buy BTC → lever up). At $78K, this implies potential BTC sales from the most leveraged Bitcoin proxy on public markets.
items.angle: When the bull case vehicle starts de-leveraging, watch the premium to NAV. If MSTR starts selling BTC to retire converts, the perpetual bid from the strategy collapses and the premium disappears. Stock trading at 2x NAV + forced BTC selling is a compounding negative.
items.tweetA: MicroStrategy is running a $1.5B buyback of its 2029 convertibles — funded by cash OR Bitcoin sales. The most leveraged BTC proxy on public markets just became a potential seller. At $78K this isn't FUD. This is the math of a leveraged vehicle forced to de-lever. Watch the premium to NAV. $MSTR
items.tweetB: MSTR's playbook was: issue converts, buy BTC, never sell. The 2029 convert buyback flips it. They might sell BTC at below average cost to retire debt. This is what forced de-leveraging looks like before it becomes forced selling. $MSTR premium to NAV should compress. $BTC

items.title: Intesa Sanpaolo $235M crypto exposure in Q1: BTC + ETH + XRP; cut Solana
items.idea: Italy's largest bank disclosed $235M in crypto positions in Q1: Bitcoin, Ethereum, and XRP. They cut Solana from the portfolio. This is a clear institutional crypto asset allocation framework: BTC for store-of-value, ETH for smart contracts, XRP for payments rails, Solana excluded. An Italian bank holding more crypto than most dedicated crypto funds.
items.angle: The Intesa Solana cut while keeping XRP is a specific institutional statement about which L1s clear the custody/regulatory bar for European banks. XRP's legal clarity post-SEC settlement makes it the preferred payments-oriented crypto for TradFi.
items.tweetA: Italy's largest bank (Intesa Sanpaolo) is sitting on $235M in crypto in Q1. BTC, ETH, XRP — and they CUT Solana. That's not just holdings data. That's a European bank telling you which crypto assets clear their regulatory/custody bar. XRP clears. SOL doesn't. Yet.
items.tweetB: European TradFi crypto adoption is not a 2027 story. Intesa Sanpaolo: $235M crypto, Q1 2026. The institutional barbell is BTC (store-of-value) + XRP (payments rails) + ETH (smart contracts). Solana trimmed. The $SOL institutional custody narrative has some ground to make up. $XRP $ETH $BTC

items.title: Modi UAE visit: ADNOC oil storage expansion to 30M bbls for India strategic reserve — signals months-long Hormuz closure expected
items.idea: Indian PM Modi is in UAE where ADNOC is expanding oil storage to 30 million barrels specifically for India's strategic reserve. India imports ~85% of its oil. A 30M bbl reserve at ADNOC provides roughly 30 days of buffer under a full Hormuz closure. Building this now signals India's assessment that the closure lasts months, not weeks.
items.angle: India building 30M bbl emergency oil storage at a UAE facility is the most consequential energy security move of the Hormuz crisis. The implicit bet: this closure lasts 60-90 days minimum before resolution. India's sovereign energy team reading on duration deserves serious weight.
items.tweetA: Modi in UAE. ADNOC expanding oil storage to 30M bbls for India's strategic reserve. India imports 85% of its oil. Building 30 days of buffer at a UAE facility means New Delhi expects Hormuz stays closed for months. The smart money on duration is India's sovereign energy team. $OIL
items.tweetB: India is pre-positioning 30M bbls of oil storage at ADNOC in UAE — roughly 30 days of import buffer under Hormuz closure. The implicit bet is this closure lasts longer than Western markets are pricing. India's read on Hormuz duration deserves serious weight.

items.title: NousResearch + xAI: x_search live for Hermes — native X search in local AI analyst at $0.10/day
items.idea: NousResearch partnered with xAI to bring x_search natively into Hermes. Users with a Grok subscription can now give Hermes native X-search capability comparable to SuperGrok. 0xJeff cut his daily research workflow cost from $0.50/day to $0.10/day. The x_search tool automatically defaults to Grok 4.3. Optimal architecture: x_search for real-time X data, Cookie MCP for on-chain structured data, DeepSeek v4 for synthesis.
items.angle: The personal analyst stack is converging rapidly on something that rivals institutional research infrastructure at $40/year in inference costs vs $200K+ for a human analyst.
items.tweetA: NousResearch + xAI: x_search is now live in Hermes. Your local AI analyst can natively search X like SuperGrok. 0xJeff cut his daily research agent cost from $0.50 to $0.10/day. The open-source analyst stack just got materially more capable.
items.tweetB: The converging personal analyst stack: x_search (Grok 4.3) for real-time X data → Cookie MCP for on-chain structured data → DeepSeek v4 for synthesis. Total cost: ~$0.10/day. What a hedge fund pays $200K/year for an analyst to do is being replicated for under $40/year in inference costs.

items.title: Grok 4.3 unusable for agent workflows; DeepSeek v4 consistently superior — specialization, not substitution
items.idea: 0xJeff tested Grok 4.3 as the base model for his 6-stage research agent pipeline. Results: crashed on browser harness, asked for confirmation at every step, never completed pipeline end-to-end. DeepSeek v4 runs the entire pipeline cleanly. Optimal architecture: Grok 4.3 only for x_search, DeepSeek handles all agent orchestration and reasoning.
items.angle: The gap between model benchmark hype and real-world agent utility is underpriced. xAI is valued partly on Grok benchmark scores. But if Grok 4.3 can't complete multi-step agent tasks without constant intervention, the enterprise agent market goes to whoever builds reliable orchestration.
items.tweetA: I keep seeing Grok 4.3 benchmark scores. 0xJeff spent a week trying to use it as an agent base model. It crashed on browser harness repeatedly, asked for confirmation at every step, never completed a pipeline. DeepSeek v4 ran it clean. Benchmark ≠ utility. The agent race is not won by single-turn IQ.
items.tweetB: Optimal AI agent stack per 0xJeff: Grok 4.3 for x_search only → DeepSeek v4 for everything else. The best models at search aren't the best models at reasoning. The best reasoning models can't access the right data. Specialization is the architecture. One-model-to-rule-them-all is the wrong bet.

items.title: Tokenized RWAs top $30B market cap — up 10x in 2 years; nearly half in US Treasury debt
items.idea: a16z crypto chart of the week: tokenized real-world assets surged from $3B to $30B in two years — 10x increase. Largest single category is tokenized US Treasury debt at nearly half the total. Traditional investors get Treasury yields with blockchain-native settlement and composability, without smart contract risk from volatile DeFi. $30B is still <0.2% of the $25T+ Treasury market.
items.angle: The Treasuries-on-chain story is where institutional DeFi is actually winning. If $30B goes to $300B (still <2% penetration), demand for stablecoin infrastructure, settlement rails, and custody grows proportionally. CLARITY Act's primary beneficiary.
items.tweetA: a16z crypto chart of the week: tokenized RWAs hit $30B — up 10x in 2 years. Nearly half is US Treasury debt. The boring version of crypto is the one winning institutional money. Not memecoins. Not L2s. Tokenized T-bills. That tells you where institutional DeFi adoption actually is.
items.tweetB: $30B in tokenized RWAs sounds big. The US Treasury market is $25T. That's 0.12% penetration. If CLARITY Act passes and institutional custody gets cleared, the path to $300B is the next 3 years. The infrastructure that wins: stablecoin rails + tokenization protocols + regulated custody.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: 2-year bar chart showing tokenized RWA growth $3B to $30B with category breakdown (Treasury debt dominant). The composition shift tells the institutional adoption story.

items.title: Farming worst year in recent memory: historic Kansas dust storms, drought, wildfires — wheat and corn starting week higher
items.idea: MacroEdge's 'On the Ground' series from Amarillo (nearly 30 pages) documents an exceptionally difficult year for US farming: drought, historic dust storms in Kansas, wildfires across the plains. Wheat and corn starting the week higher. Combined with $107 oil (diesel/fertilizer cost shock) and rents turning higher, agricultural stress is a compounding CPI input.
items.angle: Farming stress has two vectors: supply (lower yields from drought/fire) and cost (diesel at elevated prices increases input costs). Both are inflationary. The 1973 analog keeps getting stronger — that cycle also had simultaneous agricultural and energy supply shocks.
items.tweetA: MacroEdge from Amarillo: 2026 farming is brutal. Historic Kansas dust storms. Drought. Wildfires. Wheat and corn ticking higher. Add $107 diesel and Hormuz-driven fertilizer cost shocks. The agriculture supply story is quietly compounding the inflation setup nobody wants to talk about yet.
items.tweetB: The 1973 analog: simultaneous oil shock + agricultural supply disruption + shelter inflation turning + monetary policy stuck. MacroEdge is drawing it explicitly. We have all four inputs active in May 2026. The tail risk isn't 'does inflation stay hot' — it's 'how long and how hot.' $WEAT $CORN

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (On the Ground series)

items.title: 6-step Claude agent pipeline for VC investor research — 2 minutes vs 2 hours of manual prep per meeting
items.idea: The Founder Corner built a 6-step Claude agent pipeline for founder investor research: Fund Profile → Partner Background → Public Views → Portfolio Signals → Objection Map → One-Page Briefing. Key stats: VCs see 1,000+ introductions/year, take 200 meetings, invest in 4. Average VC spends 3m44s on a pitch deck (DocSend). The pipeline generates meeting-specific investor briefs in 2-3 minutes including partner's stated objections from podcasts, portfolio patterns, and story framing for the specific investor. Prompts 3-6 are behind paywall.

The Founder Corner built a 6-step Claude agent pipeline: Fund Profile → Partner Background → Public Views → Portfolio Signals → Objection Map → Briefing. VCs see 1,000+ intros/year, invest in 4. Average 3m44s spent on a deck (DocSend). The agent generates full meeting-specific investor prep in 2-3 minutes, replacing 2 hours of manual research per investor.
items.angle: The information asymmetry in fundraising has historically favored VCs. An agent that generates partner-specific, objection-specific prep in 2 minutes compresses what took 2 hours and removes the advantage. Founders who use this vs those who don't will have measurably different conversion rates.
items.tweetA: VC sees 1,000 pitches, takes 200 meetings, writes 4 checks. Average time on a deck: 3 minutes 44 seconds. A 6-step Claude agent now generates a full investor brief in 2 minutes — partner's stated objections, portfolio patterns, the right story for THIS room. The prep asymmetry just got leveled.
items.tweetB: Most founders research the fund. The ones who raise research the PARTNER — their podcast takes on API dependency risk, the portfolio write-off that mirrors your business. Claude agents now do this in 2 minutes for every meeting on your list. The information edge in fundraising just changed.
